title: Action Intent — does `level` model outcome-role (disposition), or does affirmative/dismissive deserve its own axis?
summary: Prep research for decision #1324 (raised during #1318, the `variant` axis). Action Intent (we:src/_data/intents/action.json) documents `level` (primary|secondary|tertiary|destructive, lines 10-18) as 'semantic weight determining visual prominence' — but primary/secondary buttons also carry an OUTCOME-ROLE (primary=continue/confirm, secondary=back/cancel) prominence does not capture. The question: does action DISPOSITION deserve its own modelling, or does `level` adequately stand in? FINDING 1 — 'outcome-role' is TWO separable things and one is already native: (i) BEHAVIORAL OUTCOME (submit/reset/dismiss/back) is fully expressed by the web platform — <button type=submit|reset>, <form method=dialog>+button value -> returnValue ('cancel' is canonical), and the Invoker Commands API command=show-modal|close|request-close+commandfor (request-close fires the dialog cancel event, no JS) — and Action Intent ALREADY pulls this in via requiresCapabilities:['invokers'] (we:src/_data/intents/action.json:4-6). So WE must NOT re-model behavior (intents-UX-only; that's the block/native job). (ii) DISPOSITION — the abstract affirmative-vs-dismissive-vs-neutral semantic independent of which native mechanism enacts it — is the part `level` is overloaded to carry. FINDING 2 — every native toolkit models disposition as an axis SEPARATE from prominence: SwiftUI ButtonRole={cancel,destructive} (no 'confirm' — the affirmative is the preferred/default action), Android Material AlertDialog positive/negative/neutral, WinUI ContentDialog Primary/Secondary/Close + DefaultButton{None,Primary,Secondary,Close}. SwiftUI's cancel role DRIVES ORDERING (pushed to bottom), not prominence; and the affirmative is frequently NOT the most prominent (in a destructive-confirm dialog the safe 'Cancel' is often the default/high-weight button) — so prominence cannot stand in for disposition (the same orthogonality proof #1318 used for variant). FINDING 3 (decisive, in-tree) — the gap is concrete: action.json already declares groupOrdering: dom|reverse|platform (we:src/_data/intents/action.json:26-32). `platform` ordering (macOS/iOS affirmative-last, Windows affirmative-first) is UN-IMPLEMENTABLE without knowing which action is affirmative — DOM order + `level` do not say — so groupOrdering:platform is a declared-but-unsatisfiable value, and default-button emphasis (WinUI DefaultButton, native autofocus/ENTER) is blocked the same way. This is the fork-existence proof: '`level` already stands in' is the BROKEN branch for the platform-ordering use-case. FINDING 4 — WE precedent mirrors #1318 exactly: add the orthogonal UX axis as a new dimension on the owning intent rather than overloading `level`. CLASSIFICATION: intent layer, dimension (not protocol/capability), expose whole axis, all values legitimate simultaneous end-states, explicit per-action (NOT DI/ambient), most-permissive default = unspecified (block infers from native type/command/value else neutral), seam = consumed by Action's own group axis (groupOrdering:platform) + intent->native wiring. RULING SHAPE: invariant A `level` stays prominence-only (null hypothesis rejected); invariant B behavioral outcome stays native; Fork 1 ADD a thin `disposition` dimension (affirmative|dismissive|neutral) vs leave un-modeled+infer-from-native (broken — not recoverable from a plain type=button+JS action); Fork 2 minimal abstract triple `affirmative|dismissive|neutral` (open-numbered per #1318 contract) vs literal continue|confirm|cancel|back enum (reproduces the behavior-vs-disposition conflation). OUT OF SCOPE (carved): whether `destructive` migrates off `level` to a role axis (SwiftUI models it as ButtonRole) — touches a shipped `level` value, belongs to the broader level-overload cleanup. Full grounding in we:reports/2026-06-20-action-disposition-outcome-role.md.
